title: Props and one-way data flow

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Props and one-way data flow; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Props and one-way data flow
sources.claim: Props carry read-only data from parent to child and changes cause a new render description.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Props carry read-only data from parent to child and changes cause a new render description.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A CourseCard receives title and progress and requests changes through a callback rather than editing props. Your goal is to trace data and event flow across a parent-child component pair. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Components and JSX; Function components; javascript functions parameters. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Props carry read-only data from parent to child and changes cause a new render description. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Objects can still be mutated outside React, destructuring does not clone, and excessive prop drilling can signal boundary problems. This draft therefore rejects: A child may assign to props because they are ordinary JavaScript function parameters.

includes: Treat props as read-only inputs and send changes upward through callbacks.
Trace data and event flow across a parent-child component pair.
excludes: Objects can still be mutated outside React, destructuring does not clone, and excessive prop drilling can signal boundary problems.

outcomes: Trace data and event flow across a parent-child component pair.

examples.title: Props and one-way data flow: concrete case
examples.situation: A CourseCard receives title and progress and requests changes through a callback rather than editing props.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A CourseCard receives title and progress and requests changes through a callback rather than editing props.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Treat props as read-only inputs and ask the owning component to update state through an explicit callback.
examples.result: The result follows because Treat props as read-only inputs and ask the owning component to update state through an explicit callback.

examples.title: Props and one-way data flow: boundary case
examples.situation: Objects can still be mutated outside React, destructuring does not clone, and excessive prop drilling can signal boundary problems.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: A child may assign to props because they are ordinary JavaScript function parameters.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Treat props as read-only inputs and ask the owning component to update state through an explicit callback.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Props and one-way data flow, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Props and one-way data flow before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.
